steps.specialNote: رَجُل is singular (one man). The dual is رَجُلَانِ (two men). The plural رِجَال (men) has a different pattern — called a broken plural.

ayahCards.arabicText: وَجَاءَ مِنْ أَقْصَى الْمَدِينَةِ رَجُلٌ يَسْعَىٰ
ayahCards.translation: And a man came from the farthest end of the city, running.

steps.specialNote: اِمْرَأَة (woman) is the feminine counterpart of رَجُل (man). Notice it ends in ة — the feminine marker.

ayahCards.arabicText: إِذْ قَالَتِ امْرَأَتُ عِمْرَانَ رَبِّ إِنِّي نَذَرْتُ لَكَ مَا فِي بَطْنِي
ayahCards.translation: When the wife of Imran said, 'My Lord, I have pledged to You what is in my womb.'

steps.specialNote: وَلَد means child or son. The root و-ل-د appears in Surah Al-Ikhlas: لَمْ يَلِدْ وَلَمْ يُولَدْ — 'He neither begets nor was begotten.'

ayahCards.translation: He neither begets nor was He begotten.

steps.question: The ـَانِ ending on رَجُلَانِ indicates what number?
steps.explanation: The ـَانِ (or ـَيْنِ in genitive) ending marks the dual — exactly two of something. Arabic has three numbers: singular (one), dual (two), and plural (three or more).
